title: Palaquium gutta
intro: Palaquium gutta, also known as gutta-percha, is a plant species belonging to the Sapotaceae family, native to Southeast Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, diarrhea, and skin infections, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Palaquium gutta include gutta-percha resin, which is rich in polycyclic compounds, as well as flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids. The most common herbal preparations made with Palaquium gutta include resin extracts and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including wounds, burns, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Palaquium gutta, a species of plant in the family Sapotaceae, is classified as a member of the order Ebenales. This classification is based on its characteristics, such as its large, evergreen leaves and its ability to produce a latex sap. The plant's classification is also influenced by its growth habit, which is typically a large tree or shrub. As a member of the Sapotaceae family, Palaquium gutta is closely related to other plants in this family, such as the sapodilla tree. The exact classification of Palaquium gutta may vary depending on the source and the specific botanical criteria used.
uses: Palaquium gutta, also known as the Gutta Percha tree, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes.
